lesson.menu_desc: Plans, predictions and intentions — going to, will and the present continuous for the future.
lesson.subtitle: Aula 14 — Talking About the Future
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Daniela Feitoza | Aula 14 -- Talking About the Future

listenings.text: Hi Daniela, it is Sarah, your English coach. Today, listen to one short voice note about talking about the future at work. English has three easy ways to do it. First, use going to for a plan you already decided. We are going to run a pilot in two stores. The plan is in your head, so you say going to. Second, use will for a prediction or a decision you make at the moment. I think the new system will save us time. I will send the report now. Notice, after will, you use the base verb. Say will send, never will to send. Third, use the present continuous for a fixed arrangement with a time. We are meeting the vendor on Friday. The kickoff is on Monday. Try this. When the plan is decided, going to. When you predict or decide now, will. When it is in the calendar, present continuous. Talk soon.

Hello Daniela, this is Marcus. I am leaving you a quick voicemail about the kickoff week. So, here is the plan. We are going to start the pilot and set up the two stores first. The kickoff is on Monday at ten, in the main room, and we are presenting the projection to the board on Thursday. Those dates are fixed, so please keep them free. For the first deliverable, I think your team will finish the checkout screen on time. The forecast looks good, and I anticipate only small issues. If anything goes wrong, our contingency is the old system, so we are safe. I predict the pilot will go well, and the forecast will improve after launch. Call me back when you can. Thanks, Daniela. Talk soon.
